stories.title: Monaco GP Weekend Preview — The Crown Jewel Starts Friday
stories.angle: Everything you need to know before the Monaco Grand Prix kicks off this Friday — schedule, favorites, and why 2026 regulations reshape the race

x_posts.label: Post 1
x_posts.text:
🏎️ MONACO WEEKEND IS HERE.

FP1: Friday June 5 | 7:30 AM ET
Qualifying: Saturday June 6 | 9:00 AM ET
Race: Sunday June 7 | 9:00 AM ET | Apple TV+

Ferrari favored. Antonelli's streak on the line. Verstappen's exit clause ticking.

#F1 #MonacoGP #F1Fanrecap

facebook.long_form:
The Monaco Grand Prix is this weekend — and in 2026, it arrives with more drama than any edition in recent memory.

Formula 1's most iconic race takes place on the streets of Monte Carlo, and this year the field is more unpredictable than it has been in years. Here is everything you need to know:

The schedule: Practice 1 begins Friday June 5 at 7:30 AM ET. Practice 2 follows at 11:00 AM ET. Qualifying runs Saturday June 6 at 9:00 AM ET. The race is Sunday June 7 at 9:00 AM ET on Apple TV+ (US coverage).

What makes this Monaco different: New for 2026, the FIA has banned the "Straight Mode" active aerodynamics system at Monaco. No section of the circuit meets the 3-second activation threshold, so all teams compete without the variable wing advantage that has defined the season so far. That removes Mercedes' straight-line speed edge and opens the door for Ferrari and McLaren to compete more equally.

The championship is real: Kimi Antonelli leads by 43 points after winning four of the five races so far. George Russell, Charles Leclerc, Lewis Hamilton, and Lando Norris are all still mathematically in the fight. Monaco is the kind of track where championships shift.

Don't miss a session this weekend.
facebook.image_caption: Monaco Grand Prix 2026. Practice starts Friday. Qualifying Saturday. Race Sunday 9 AM ET on Apple TV+. Ferrari, Mercedes, McLaren — this one could go anywhere.

article.headline: Monaco GP 2026 Weekend Guide: Schedule, Favorites, and Why This One Is Different

stories.title: Ferrari's Best Chance for a 2026 Win — Leclerc and Hamilton Eye Monaco Glory
stories.angle: Ferrari is favored at Monaco — Leclerc 2/1, Hamilton 4/1 — as SF-26 low-speed excellence and active-aero ban remove Mercedes' advantage

x_posts.label: Post 2-1
x_posts.text:
Ferrari. Has. Not. Won. A. Race. In. 2026.

That changes at Monaco.

Leclerc 2/1 favorite. Hamilton 4/1. Active aero ban removes Mercedes' engine edge.

Leclerc defending champion. Hamilton: "Power is not king there."

Time. #F1 #MonacoGP #Ferrari

x_posts.label: Post 2-2
x_posts.text:
5 races in 2026:
→ Antonelli: 4 wins
→ Mercedes: 5 wins
→ Ferrari wins: 0

Monaco F1 GP odds this weekend:
Leclerc 2/1 | Antonelli 4/1 | Hamilton 4/1

SF-26 LOVES slow corners. Monaco is 90% slow corners.

#F1 #MonacoGP #Ferrari #F1Fanrecap

facebook.long_form:
Formula 1 has run five races in 2026. Mercedes has won all five. Ferrari has won zero.

Monaco is where that changes.

Charles Leclerc enters as the 2/1 bookmakers' favorite — not because of any result, but because of what Ferrari's SF-26 does in slow corners, which is essentially what Monaco is. The 2026 active aero ban removes the engine and straight-line speed advantage that has powered Mercedes to five consecutive wins. On the streets of Monte Carlo, mechanical grip and traction are king — and Ferrari has that in abundance.

Leclerc should be deeply motivated. He won here in 2024. He has three Monaco poles to his name — more than any other active driver. He grew up in nearby Fontvieille in the Principality of Monaco. This is not just any race for him.

Lewis Hamilton makes it a genuine Ferrari double threat. The seven-time champion finished second at the Canadian Grand Prix — the best result of his Ferrari career so far — and has spoken candidly about Monaco's unique characteristics. "Power is not king there," Hamilton said. "I think our car could be really strong." Hamilton has won three Monaco Grands Prix in his career, but never in a Ferrari.

For the first time in 2026, Mercedes is not the team to beat. Ferrari's window is open.
facebook.image_caption: Leclerc 2/1. Hamilton 4/1. Ferrari hasn't won in 2026 — until this weekend. The SF-26 was built for Monaco and the active aero ban removes Mercedes' edge. This is Ferrari's race to lose.

article.headline: Ferrari's Best Chance of 2026: Why Leclerc and Hamilton Can Finally Win at Monaco

stories.title: Can Antonelli Make It Five in a Row? His Monaco Test Begins
stories.angle: Antonelli arrives with 43-point lead and 4 straight wins — but Monaco is his hardest test yet with Ferrari and even Russell hungry for points

x_posts.label: Post 3
x_posts.text:
Kimi Antonelli enters Monaco:

→ 4 wins from 5 races (F1 first)
→ 43-point championship lead
→ Youngest F1 points leader EVER

And his own team's main man admits: "Ferrari will be the team to beat."

Still think he's unbeatable? #F1 #MonacoGP #Antonelli #F1Fanrecap

facebook.long_form:
Kimi Antonelli walks into Monaco as the most dominant points leader in Formula 1 in a decade. Four wins from five races. The youngest championship leader in the sport's history. A 43-point cushion over the field. He is 19 years old.

And for the first time this season, he is not the favorite.

Antonelli himself acknowledged it: Ferrari, he said, "will be the team to beat" at Monaco. The circuit's emphasis on low-speed traction, mechanical grip, and precision over raw power gives Ferrari and the SF-26 an inherent edge that even the W17 cannot match on these streets.

That is not a crisis for Antonelli. It is a test.

He has become the youngest championship points leader in F1 history. He won in Australia, won in Japan, won in Miami, and won again in Canada — making himself the first driver since the sport's early era to convert his first four pole positions into four consecutive victories. His consistency has been extraordinary.

Monaco will require something different: resilience under adversity, the ability to finish second when he cannot win, and the mental strength to protect a championship lead rather than chase a fifth victory.

At 19, in his first real title battle, that may be the hardest thing he has ever done.
facebook.image_caption: 4 wins. 43-point lead. Youngest champion-in-waiting ever. But at Monaco — even Antonelli's own team says Ferrari will be the team to beat. His biggest test starts Friday.

article.headline: Kimi Antonelli Arrives at Monaco as Champion-in-Waiting — Can He Hold On When He's Not the Favorite?

stories.title: Verstappen's Exit Clause Ticking — Monaco Is His Last Real Lifeline
stories.angle: Verstappen sits 7th in the standings with the summer-break exit clause approaching July 27; Monaco may be his best shot at proving Red Bull can still fight

x_posts.label: Post 4
x_posts.text:
Max Verstappen. Monaco. Exit clause.

He sits 7th in the standings. The clause triggers if he's outside top 2 by July 27.

He won Monaco in 2021. And 2023.

Can he do it again — when he NEEDS it most? #F1 #MonacoGP #Verstappen #F1Fanrecap

facebook.long_form:
Max Verstappen inserted a very specific clause into his Red Bull contract before the 2026 season began. If he is outside the top two in the Drivers' Championship when the summer break starts on July 27, he has the right to walk away from the team.

He currently sits seventh.

The clause was added out of "apprehension" about Red Bull's new power unit — the RBPT3 — which was developed under the 2026 regulations. That apprehension has proven justified. The Red Bull RB21 has been competitive but not dominant. Verstappen's best result so far is a third-place finish at the Canadian Grand Prix — Red Bull's first podium of the 2026 season.

Monaco is a special case for Verstappen. He won here in 2021 and again in 2023. The Circuit de Monaco rewards driver craft, experience, and the ability to extract lap time from a car's mechanical limits rather than its raw performance. Verstappen has demonstrated exactly those skills on these streets.

But the mathematics are severe. He needs to make up 45 points on George Russell — who sits second in the standings — in the next three races before the summer break. Even if Verstappen wins Monaco and Russell scores zero, the gap barely closes.

What Monaco can do is give Red Bull evidence that the RB21 can fight. If Verstappen wins, suddenly the July deadline feels less like a farewell and more like a countdown.
facebook.image_caption: Verstappen won Monaco in 2021. And in 2023. Now, with his exit clause ticking and Red Bull struggling, Monaco may be his most important race yet. The deadline is July 27.

article.headline: Verstappen's Monaco Lifeline: The Exit Clause, the Deadline, and the Track He's Won Twice

stories.title: Russell at Monaco — 43 Points Down, Nothing to Lose
stories.angle: Russell trails Antonelli by 43 after his Canada retirement — his first Monaco win could reshape the title fight before the summer

x_posts.label: Post 5
x_posts.text:
George Russell was LEADING Canada when the power unit failed on Lap 30.

He now trails Antonelli by 43 points.

Monaco starts Friday. He has never won there.

This weekend matters more than any so far. #F1 #MonacoGP #Russell #F1Fanrecap

facebook.long_form:
George Russell was in total control of the Canadian Grand Prix. He had taken pole, survived a dramatic battle with Antonelli in the opening stint, and had the race in his hands when his power unit failed on Lap 30.

He went from leading to not-classified in a single moment.

The championship gap jumped from 18 points to 43. Russell flew home from Montreal and has spent the two weeks since doing the only productive thing available: preparing for Monaco.

He has never won the Monaco Grand Prix. In a career that already includes race wins, a World Championship race, and consistent podiums, it is the one obvious gap on his résumé. This weekend, the personal and the strategic align perfectly — Monaco is exactly the race where Russell can score maximum points, demonstrate his speed on a circuit that rewards raw qualifying pace, and narrow Antonelli's lead in a single afternoon.

The complication: Mercedes is not favored this weekend. The active aero ban removes the power unit advantage that has been their margin all season. Russell will have to extract more from his car here than anywhere in 2026 to challenge Ferrari on equal terms.

He has done it before. Montreal, where he took pole by 0.068 seconds, showed he can find the last thousandth of a second under pressure.

The question is whether the W17 can give him enough to fight with this weekend.
facebook.image_caption: Russell led Canada. His power unit failed on Lap 30. He's now 43 points behind Antonelli. Monaco starts Friday — and Russell has never won here. This weekend is not optional for his championship.

article.headline: Russell's 43-Point Deficit, a Maiden Monaco Win, and One Last Chance to Make the Title Fight Real
